narrative: You spot a flat rosette of broad oval leaves growing at the edge of the woodland path...
interesting_fact: Native Americans called it White Man's Footprint because it spread wherever European settlers went — following their paths and roadsides across the world.

questions.question: Which of these plants is Greater Burdock?
questions.remember: Greater Burdock has larger flower heads on long spreading stalks and solid leaf stalks. Lesser Burdock is more compact with clustered stalkless flower heads and hollow leaf stalks — cut across the stalk to check

questions.question: Does Broadleaf Plantain have any other common names?
options: White Man's Footprint
Goosegrass
Alleluia
Winterweed
questions.remember: Native Americans called it White Man's Footprint because it spread wherever European settlers went — following their paths and roadsides across the world

questions.question: What is the botanical name of Broadleaf Plantain?
options: Plantago major
Plantago lanceolata
Plantago media
Plantago maritima

questions.question: Where is Broadleaf Plantain typically found?
options: Paths, lawns, disturbed and compacted ground throughout the UK
Damp woodland edges and hedgerows on heavy clay soil
Meadows and grassland on well-drained chalky soils
Field margins and arable land on fertile loamy soil
questions.correct: Paths, lawns, disturbed and compacted ground throughout the UK

questions.question: Select all key identifying features of Broadleaf Plantain
options: Large oval leaves in a flat rosette with prominent parallel veins
Broken stem reveals string-like fibres similar to celery
Strong minty smell when crushed
Finely toothed leaf edges with a slightly wavy margin
Tiny hooked hairs that cling to clothing
correct: Large oval leaves in a flat rosette with prominent parallel veins
Broken stem reveals string-like fibres similar to celery

questions.question: What is Broadleaf Plantain used for? Select all that apply
options: Young leaves eaten raw or cooked
Seeds ground into flour
Made into a tea
Roots boiled and eaten as a starchy vegetable
correct: Young leaves eaten raw or cooked

questions.question: What does Broadleaf Plantain taste like?
options: Bitter and slightly vegetal — young leaves are milder and best eaten in spring
Mild and sweet with a slightly mucilaginous texture similar to cooked okra
Sharp and tangy with a lemony finish due to oxalic acid in the leaves
Earthy and nutty — similar in flavour to cooked spinach or chard
questions.correct: Bitter and slightly vegetal — young leaves are milder and best eaten in spring

questions.question: What are the cautions with Broadleaf Plantain?
options: No known adverse reactions — avoid older leaves as they become very fibrous and tough
Contains oxalic acid — avoid in large quantities especially if prone to kidney stones
Safe in small amounts but may cause digestive upset if eaten in quantity due to tannin content
Avoid during pregnancy as it has historically been used to stimulate uterine contractions
questions.correct: No known adverse reactions — avoid older leaves as they become very fibrous and tough

questions.question: Which species could Broadleaf Plantain be confused with?
options: Ribwort Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) — similar parallel veins and flower spike but narrow lance-shaped leaves
Foxglove (Digitalis purpurea) — similar large oval leaves but matte and hairy with no parallel veins
Greater Burdock (Arctium lappa) — similar broad leaves but much larger with a woolly underside
Common Sorrel (Rumex acetosa) — similar rosette habit but arrow-shaped leaves with pointed lobes
questions.correct: Ribwort Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) — similar parallel veins and flower spike but narrow lance-shaped leaves

questions.question: Is Broadleaf Plantain native to the UK?
options: Yes — native throughout Britain and Ireland
No — introduced from Europe during the Roman period as a medicinal herb
Yes — but only in England and Wales, rarely found in Scotland
No — introduced from Asia in the 17th century and now naturalised
questions.correct: Yes — native throughout Britain and Ireland

questions.question: What was Broadleaf Plantain historically used to treat? Select all that apply
options: Insect stings and bites
Minor wounds and cuts
Burns
Broken bones

questions.question: How was Broadleaf Plantain applied medicinally? Select all that apply
options: Fresh leaves crushed and applied directly as a poultice
Made into a tea or infusion
Burned and inhaled as smoke
Ground into a powder and swallowed dry
correct: Fresh leaves crushed and applied directly as a poultice
Made into a tea or infusion

questions.question: What is the life cycle of Broadleaf Plantain?
options: Perennial — returns every year from the same rootstock
Annual — completes its full life cycle within a single growing season
Biennial — produces a leaf rosette in the first year and flowers in the second
Ephemeral — appears briefly after rain then dies back completely within weeks
questions.correct: Perennial — returns every year from the same rootstock

questions.question: What plant family does Broadleaf Plantain belong to?
options: Plantaginaceae — the plantain family
Rosaceae — the rose family
Asteraceae — the daisy family
Lamiaceae — the mint family

questions.question: What is the origin of the name Plantago?
options: Possibly from Latin planta meaning sole of the foot — possibly referring to its flat rosette shape
Possibly from Latin plantare meaning to plant — possibly referring to its use in early cultivation
Possibly from Greek platos meaning flat — possibly referring to the broad shape of the leaves
Possibly from Celtic plann meaning spread — possibly referring to how widely it grows
questions.correct: Possibly from Latin planta meaning sole of the foot — possibly referring to its flat rosette shape

questions.question: What does Major mean in the botanical name Plantago major?
options: Greater or larger in Latin — distinguishing it from smaller plantain species like Plantago media
Medicinal in Latin — referring to its long history of use as a healing herb
Common in Latin — referring to how widespread and abundant it is across Europe
Ancient in Latin — referring to how long it has been used by humans as food and medicine
questions.correct: Greater or larger in Latin — distinguishing it from smaller plantain species like Plantago media
